content:
===BEGIN===
Large language models (LLMs) generate text through autoregressive token-by-token prediction based on a learned probability distribution over sequences. At their core, transformer architectures leverage self-attention mechanisms to contextualize each token relative to all prior tokens, enabling nuanced capture of long-range dependencies and hierarchical structure in language. During training, models optimize cross-entropy loss over vast corpora, internalizing statistical patterns and semantic associations without explicit symbolic rules. Generation proceeds by conditioning on a prompt and sampling subsequent tokens from the model’s output distribution, often using techniques such as temperature scaling or nucleus sampling to balance creativity and coherence. Crucially, the model’s internal representations encode multifaceted linguistic information—syntax, semantics, pragmatics—emerging from distributed embeddings and multi-head attentions, rather than discrete linguistic categories. This probabilistic framework allows LLMs to produce contextually appropriate continuations that reflect learned distributions rather than deterministic linguistic rules, enabling flexibility but also introducing challenges related to ambiguity, bias, and factuality in generated text.

===END===
